source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure Monitoring and Alerting is Enabled

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure Monitoring and Alerting is Enabled
input_row.description: Implementing monitoring and alerting on Amazon MemoryDB allows you to proactively detect and respond to any performance issues, security events, or operational anomalies.
input_row.rationale: This helps in ensuring that everything in the system is secure and if there is an unusual activity that takes place it addresses the issues quickly and efficiently. Impact: Enabling monitoring and alerting has a positive impact in the business operations when the issue is identified and addressed accordingly.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign into the AWS Management Console • Sign into the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon MemoryDB Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/memorydb/. 3. Select the Cluster • Choose the Amazon MemoryDB cluster for which you want to implement monitoring and alerting. Click on the cluster name to access its details page. 4. Enable Amazon CloudWatch • In the cluster details page, navigate to the Monitoring or CloudWatch section. • Click on Enable to enable CloudWatch monitoring for the cluster. • Select the appropriate CloudWatch metric categories to monitor, such as CPU utilization, memory utilization, network traffic, and storage capacity. • Configure the desired granularity and period for metric collection. • Click Enable or Save to enable CloudWatch monitoring for the cluster.  5. Set Up CloudWatch Alarms • In the CloudWatch console, navigate to Alarms in the left-side menu. • Click on Create Alarm to set up a new alarm. • Select the CloudWatch metric related to the aspect you want to monitor, such as CPU utilization or memory utilization. • Configure the alarm threshold based on your desired criteria, such as setting CPU utilization above a certain percentage. • Define the actions to be taken when the alarm is triggered. • Click Create Alarm to create the CloudWatch alarm. 6. Configure Amazon EventBridge Rules (Optional) • In the Amazon EventBridge console, navigate to Rules in the left-side menu. • Click on Create rule to set up a new rule. • Define the event pattern or source that should trigger the rule, such as specific MemoryDB events or errors. • Configure the target actions, such as sending notifications, executing AWS Lambda functions, or invoking AWS Step Functions. • Click Create to create the EventBridge rule. 7. Configure Auto Scaling (Optional) • In the MemoryDB cluster details page, navigate to the Auto Scaling section. • Configure auto-scaling settings based on your workload and performance requirements. • Define the scaling policies, such as increasing or decreasing the number of replica nodes based on CPU utilization or other metrics. • Set the desired minimum and maximum number of replica nodes for the cluster. • Click Save or Apply Changes to apply the auto-scaling configuration. 8. Regularly Review and Adjust Monitoring and Alarms • Periodically review the CloudWatch metrics and alarms to ensure they align with your monitoring needs and performance expectations. • Adjust the thresholds and actions based on changing workload patterns or performance requirements. • Stay informed about new CloudWatch features and best practices to optimize your monitoring setup.
input_row.impact: Enabling monitoring and alerting has a positive impact in the business operations when the issue is identified and addressed accordingly.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control to ensure monitoring and alerting is enabled for Amazon MemoryDB can be effectively audited using AWS APIs. The audit involves checking configurations related to Amazon CloudWatch and EventBridge which are integrated with AWS services and have accessible APIs. The setup includes:

- Verifying that CloudWatch monitoring is enabled for the MemoryDB cluster.
- Checking if relevant CloudWatch alarms are set up for key metrics (e.g., CPU utilization, memory utilization).
- Ensuring EventBridge rules are configured if necessary, which is an optional step but still API-checkable.
- Reviewing auto-scaling configurations through AWS APIs or the SDK.

Considering that all of these configurations can be queried using AWS APIs provided by services like boto3 for programmatically accessing AWS resources, this audit can be automated.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_memorydb_cluster_monitoring_and_alerting_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Use boto3 SDK Methods:** 
  - Utilize `boto3` to access `describe_clusters` for MemoryDB to verify that the cluster monitoring is enabled.
  - Access `get_metric_data` or `describe_alarms` from the `CloudWatch` client to ensure alarms are configured with expected thresholds and actions.
  - Query `describe_rule` using the `events` client to confirm if EventBridge rules are present for the MemoryDB cluster.
  - For auto-scaling verification, use `describe_scaling_policies` from the `application-autoscaling` client to check the presence and configuration of scaling policies.
  
- **AWS CLI Commands (if applicable):**
  - `aws memorydb describe-clusters --region <region>` to list all clusters.
  - `aws cloudwatch describe-alarms --region <region>` to review defined alarms.
  - `aws events list-rules --region <region>` to check for any EventBridge rules related to MemoryDB.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
  - Enumerate all MemoryDB clusters within each AWS region.
  - List configured CloudWatch alarms related to MemoryDB metrics.
  - Review EventBridge rules for event-driven actions.

- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - Ensure CloudWatch monitoring is enabled.
  - Confirm that alarms covering essential MemoryDB metrics are activated and properly configured.
  - Validate if any EventBridge rules relevant to the MemoryDB cluster exist.

- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** CloudWatch monitoring is enabled, relevant alarms are set up with correct thresholds, optional EventBridge rules are presiding for additional automation, and any auto-scaling policies are configured as required.
  - **Fail:** Any of the above components are missing or incorrectly configured, indicating potential gaps in monitoring and alerting the setup.

These automation steps fully leverage AWS API capabilities to ensure comprehensive checks on the configured properties for Amazon MemoryDB monitoring and alerting.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of this audit as Automated is correct. AWS APIs, boto3, and CLI commands can effectively verify the configurations for monitoring and alerting in Amazon MemoryDB.
- The use of AWS SDK capabilities, such as boto3, and AWS CLI commands is appropriate for checking the required configurations.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of `boto3` methods like `describe_clusters` for MemoryDB and `describe_alarms` for CloudWatch is valid.
- The CLI commands provided are correct for listing clusters and alarms.
- The program name `aws_memorydb_cluster_monitoring_and_alerting_configured` is descriptive but could be more specific regarding the exact checks being performed.
- The mention of EventBridge rules and auto-scaling checks is accurate, though auto-scaling might not be directly applicable to MemoryDB clusters as they do not support auto-scaling in the same way as other services like EC2.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved for clarity. A suggestion could be `aws_memorydb_monitoring_alerting_configuration_check` to better reflect the focus on configuration.
- Consider including checks for specific CloudWatch metrics that are critical for MemoryDB, such as `EngineCPUUtilization` and `FreeableMemory`.
- Clarify the role of EventBridge rules in the context of MemoryDB, as they might not be directly applicable unless specific custom events are configured.
- Auto-scaling checks should be clarified or omitted, as MemoryDB does not inherently support auto-scaling policies like EC2 or RDS.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The approach and technical details provided are sound, with minor improvements suggested for clarity and specificity.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name for clarity and specificity. Clarify the role of EventBridge and auto-scaling in the context of MemoryDB, ensuring the focus remains on applicable configurations.
- The audit can remain automated, but with the suggested modifications to improve clarity and relevance to MemoryDB's capabilities.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions agree, and AWS provides full API support (via boto3 and AWS CLI) to programmatically verify if Amazon MemoryDB clusters have CloudWatch monitoring enabled and if CloudWatch alarms are configured for key metrics. EventBridge rules are less commonly used for MemoryDB but can also be checked via API if present. No manual inspection is required, as all relevant properties are exposed through AWS APIs.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_memorydb_cluster_monitoring_and_alerting_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** Amazon MemoryDB Clusters, CloudWatch Alarms
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - `boto3.client('memorydb').describe_clusters()`  
  - `boto3.client('cloudwatch').describe_alarms()`  
  - (Optional) `boto3.client('events').list_rules()` for EventBridge
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - For each MemoryDB cluster, confirm CloudWatch monitoring is enabled (MemoryDB clusters send metrics to CloudWatch by default, but verify metrics are present).
  - Check for existence of CloudWatch alarms on critical MemoryDB metrics (e.g., `EngineCPUUtilization`, `FreeableMemory`, `CurrConnections`, `Evictions`).
  - (Optional) Check for EventBridge rules targeting MemoryDB events if custom alerting is implemented.
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - At least one CloudWatch alarm exists for each MemoryDB cluster, monitoring at least one critical metric with a defined threshold and notification action (e.g., SNS topic).
  - (Optional) EventBridge rules exist if required by organizational policy.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - No CloudWatch alarms are configured for MemoryDB clusters, or alarms do not monitor critical metrics, or no notification actions are set.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The second opinion suggested a more descriptive program name and clarified that auto-scaling is not directly applicable to MemoryDB. I have incorporated these improvements by focusing the check on monitoring and alerting, omitting auto-scaling, and using a precise program name.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
